interest: A coalition of pilot-district families wanting predictable rules, researchers wanting genuine learning rather than a pre-baked result, the STEM labour sector wanting a preserved aptitude signal, and a durability-minded government wanting a defensible low-commitment step.
value: Epistemic honesty and procedural fairness (transparent, advance-published, empirically-tested criteria) in tension with political expediency and the pressure to declare a pre-committed reform a success.
fear: That an admittedly unvalidated proxy and assessment get rolled out on a politically pre-committed timeline, blindsiding compliant families mid-application-cycle and discrediting the whole SES-aware approach.
affected: families and applicants in pilot districts
assumption: That a gaming-resistant SES proxy, a less-coachable-yet-still-predictive assessment, intensive monitoring, and criteria published a full cycle in advance are all actually achievable within the pilot's compressed timeframe and political constraints.
empirical_uncertainty: Contested-to-unknown: whether the new SES proxy resists gaming and whether the redesigned assessment still identifies genuine aptitude (versus merely different demographics) is untested, and the scenario itself flags no existing SES register exists to build the proxy on.
